coredata.dc:title: S-scheme BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalyst for efficient degradation of ofloxacin: Reactive oxygen species transformation mechanism insight
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Yuan
Wang, Zhaowei
Cai, Yonghui
Song, Mengxi
Qi, Kemin
Xie, Xiaoyun
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis technology exhibited promising application for advanced treatment of wastewater. Nevertheless, the design of efficient photocatalyst and the mechanism of free radicals in pollutant degradation still remained to be further investigated. Herein, BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalyst was fabricated by electrostatic self-assembly method, which exhibited the excellent photocatalytic performance. Under visible-light irradiation, the removal rate of ofloxacin by BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH (0.25 min−1) was 1.93 times than pristine BiVO4, and the removal efficiency in 15 min reached 99.21%. The perfect reusability of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH was ascribed to the persistent catalytic active centers provided by the renewable surface oxygen vacancies on the β-FeOOH. As electron transfer channels, CQDs facilitated the transfer of BiVO4 photogeneration electrons. The matched band structure allowed the construction of S-scheme heterojunctions, and the higher conduction band position was retained while the carrier separation was promoted. More importantly, this work firstly reported the phenomenon that the main reactive groups in the photocatalysis process would be directionally transformed with the change of pH conditions. Based on the analysis of capture and electron paramagnetic resonance experiments, ·O2 − was the main free radicals to photodegrade OFL in neutral and alkaline conditions. However, when the solution pH turned into acidic, the photodegradation of OFL was dominated by 1O2. This innovative phenomenon was due to that acidic condition accelerated the reaction kinetics of spontaneous transformation of ·O2 − to 1O2 and inhibited the direct oxidation of pollutants by ·O2 −. Accordingly, this research could inspire theoretical study of free radical reaction and the design of S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: S-scheme BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalyst for efficient degradation of ofloxacin: Reactive oxygen species transformation mechanism insight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis technology exhibited promising application for advanced treatment of wastewater. Nevertheless, the design of efficient photocatalyst and the mechanism of free radicals in pollutant degradation still remained to be further investigated. Herein, BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalyst was fabricated by electrostatic self-assembly method, which exhibited the excellent photocatalytic performance. Under visible-light irradiation, the removal rate of ofloxacin by BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH (0.25 min−1) was 1.93 times than pristine BiVO4, and the removal efficiency in 15 min reached 99.21%. The perfect reusability of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH was ascribed to the persistent catalytic active centers provided by the renewable surface oxygen vacancies on the β-FeOOH. As electron transfer channels, CQDs facilitated the transfer of BiVO4 photogeneration electrons. The matched band structure allowed the construction of S-scheme heterojunctions, and the higher conduction band position was retained while the carrier separation was promoted. More importantly, this work firstly reported the phenomenon that the main reactive groups in the photocatalysis process would be directionally transformed with the change of pH conditions. Based on the analysis of capture and electron paramagnetic resonance experiments, ·O2 − was the main free radicals to photodegrade OFL in neutral and alkaline conditions. However, when the solution pH turned into acidic, the photodegradation of OFL was dominated by 1O2. This innovative phenomenon was due to that acidic condition accelerated the reaction kinetics of spontaneous transformation of ·O2 − to 1O2 and inhibited the direct oxidation of pollutants by ·O2 −. Accordingly, this research could inspire theoretical study of free radical reaction and the design of S-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: As fluoroquinolone antibiotics are considered to be well tolerated drugs with relatively limited adverse effects, they have been used increasingly in therapy and prevention (Albini and Monti, 2003). However, the global usage of ofloxacin (OFL) and other antibiotics poses a growing threat to the environment owing to their poor biodegradability (Jin et al., 2017). Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been widely applied for the removal of refractory organic pollutants. Photocatalysis has attracted considerable attention in the past decades as a combination of degradation effect and cost effectiveness among AOPs (Loeb et al., 2019).

BiVO4 is one of the semiconductors photocatalyst with excellent performance by virtue of its superior photocatalytic activity, better chemical and thermal stability, simple preparation process and environment friendly, etc. Lone pair distortion of Bi 6s orbitals which is in monoclinic scheelite phase BiVO4, making BiVO4 exist superior activity under visible light irradiation (Malathi et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the VB position of BiVO4 is ∼2.4 V vs. NHE, which indicates that the photogenerated holes have potential to oxidize OFL (Zhao et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the above nanoparticles still fail to overcome the inherent drawbacks of a single semiconductor photocatalysts, including the low charge separation efficiency and inability to obtain wide light absorption range and strong redox capability at the same time. The photogenerated electrons and holes in a single photocatalyst are unable to resist the huge Columbic force, resulting in rapid recombination, which is the culprit of low charge separation efficiency of BiVO4. Simultaneously, the wide light absorption range and strong redox capability are irreconcilable, since the former requires narrow band gap of semiconductors while the latter requires semiconductors to have lower VB and higher CB position. The CB of BiVO4 is relatively low (∼0 V vs. NHE), which is inferior to the redox potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE). The photoelectrons with insufficient energy are far from meeting the requirements of participating in oxygen reduction reaction effectively. Therefore, there is an urgent need for an effective strategy to promote the application of BiVO4 for water pollutants photodegradation.

Fortunately, recent studies have shown that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can serve as solid-state electronic mediators to shuttle electrons between two semiconductors in the system (Tada et al., 2006; Miao et al., 2018). Compared with the traditional precious metals (such as Au, Ag) as charge transfer bridge, CQDs with a size smaller than 10 nm are more appropriate as an electron transfer channels to construct semiconductor photocatalytic with mediators because of its impressive electron transfer and electron storage (Liu et al., 2015). Therefore, CQDs are the high-performance solid-state electronic mediator, which could be introduced into semiconductor photocatalysts to regulate the migration path of photogenerated electrons (Zhang et al., 2019). Nevertheless, the combination of CQDs and BiVO4 could not adjust the band structure of BiVO4 (Huang et al., 2017). In other words, the introduction of CQDs failed to improve the redox capability of BiVO4. In this regard, the strategy of constructing heterojunctions is worth adopting.

Z-scheme heterojunction have been used to enhance the activity of semiconductor photocatalysts extensively. Nevertheless, for all-solid Z-scheme heterojunctions, the electron transfer depends on the Fermi level (EF) compared with the EF of oxidative photocatalyst (OP) and reductive photocatalyst (RP). When the EF of mediator is not positioned between the EF of the OP and RP, the electron transferred from the RP to the OP is not possible (Xu et al., 2020). The recently developed step (S)-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic system is an efficient way to promote the separation of photogenerated charges (Fu et al., 2019; F. He et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2021). When the two semiconductors are in close contact and made up the S-scheme heterojunction，since the difference in the EF of RP and OP, electrons in the RP with high EF will diffuse to the OP with low EF spontaneously until the equilibrium of EF is attained. Concurrently, an internal electric field directing from RP to OP is formed, which can accelerate the recombination of low-energy RP holes and OP electrons (Ekande et al., 2021). The innovative heterojunction system maximizes the redox potential of charge carriers and increases charge separation, which is beneficial to the degradation of antibiotics (Deng et al., 2017). Therefore, the S-scheme heterojunction can be used to make up for the deficiency of BiVO4/CQDs. Goethite (β-FeOOH) exists universally in nature, which is low-cost, corrosion resistant, non-toxic, and harmless, attracting much attention. According to previous studies, the higher oxygen vacancy (OV) density on the surface of β-FeOOH could significantly boost the activity of the composite photocatalysts (Hu et al., 2019; Asor et al., 2020). Theoretically, since the EF of BiVO4 is lower than of β-FeOOH, the electrons will be transferred from β-FeOOH to BiVO4 after close contact (Tan et al., 2017a; Yan et al., 2017a). The direction of the internal electric field is beneficial to retain the highly oxidizing holes of BiVO4 and the strong reducing electrons of β-FeOOH. Therefore, β-FeOOH and BiVO4 have great potential to form S-scheme heterojunctions. Meanwhile, the band positions of β-FeOOH and BiVO4 match wonderfully, hence β-FeOOH has enormous potential in the application of modifying BiVO4 (Huang et al., 2018; S. He et al., 2020). Consequently, it is considerably expected to deliver the merits of CQDs combined with the S-scheme heterojunction to further boost the photogenerated charge separation efficiency and regulate the band structure to overcome the defects of pristine BiVO4 catalyst actually.

In this study, a novel S-scheme BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalyst was synthesized by electrostatic self-assembly method. The structure, morphology, and optoelectronic properties of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH composite were studied in detail. The photocatalytic activity of composite was evaluated by photodegradation experiments of OFL. Through reactive species trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments under different pH conditions, the free radical generation mechanism of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH composite and the mechanism of OFL degradation were explored from a new point of view. As expected, the BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH composite showed a robust photocatalytic performance for OFL removal in ultrapure water and actual water, respectively. It mainly benefited from the existence of surface oxygen vacancy, CQDs as an electron transfer channel as well as the construction of S-scheme heterojunction.

Ofloxacin (OFL, 98%), as a target pollutant, was purchased from J&K Scientific Co., China and its basic chemical and physical properties were shown in Table S1. Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O ≥ 99.5%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) and analytical grade ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, Xilong Science Co., Ltd.), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), sodium chloride (NaCl, Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd.), and ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3, Damao Chemical Reagent Factory) were used for material synthesis. Acetonitrile and trifluoroacetic acid were HPLC grade. The above reagents were used without further purification.

The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method (Hu et al., 2021). 1.0507 g C6H8O7·H2O and 335 μL ethylenediamine were evenly mixed in 10 mL ultrapure water. The mixture solution was then transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave (KH100, Taikang Technology Co., Ltd.) and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down naturally to room temperature, the mixture solution was dialyzed for 24 h and then got CQDs solution, which was stored at 4 °C.

The β-FeOOH was prepared from FeCl3·6H2O and NaCl via a hydrothermal method (Niu et al., 2019). The 70 mL aqueous solution containing 1.9462 g FeCl3·6H2O and 2.3144 g NaCl was loaded into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was kept at 120 °C for 12 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The product was taken out and washed with ultrapure water and ethanol thoroughly, then vacuum dried at 60 °C.

BiVO4/CQDs material was prepared by hydrothermal method (Wu et al., 2017). Firstly, 2.42535 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.5849 g NH4VO3 were dissolved in 20 mL 2 M HNO3 and 10 mL 2 M NaOH, respectively. Under stirring, the latter was added into the former drop by drop, and the suspension was adjusted to neutral pH with 1 M NaOH. After stirring for 30 min, the 3 mL CQDs aqueous solution was added to the yellow suspension, followed by ultrasound for 30 min. Then the mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Finally, after the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the resultant solid was taken out, washed with ultrapure water several times, and then dried in air. Under the same conditions as above, the pristine BiVO4 sample was synthesized without adding CQDs.

BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH composites were prepared by electrostatic self-assembly of BiVO4/CQDs and β-FeOOH (Obregón et al., 2016). Under neutral conditions, the zeta potentials of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were about −57 mV and +55 mV, respectively, and they could be self-assembled depended on their strong electrostatic attraction (Ge et al., 2019a). In a typical experiment, a certain amount of β-FeOOH and BiVO4/CQDs powders were added to the agate mortar containing 2 mL anhydrous ethanol, and dispersed by ultrasonic for 5 min. Then, it was stirred and ground until the anhydrous ethanol was evaporated. Finally, the solid sample was dried in 80 °C air for 2 h. The mass ratios of β-FeOOH and BiVO4/CQDs were 10%, 30% and 50%, labeled as BCF-10, BCF-30, and BCF-50, respectively.

Characterizations were carried out using scan electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5600LV), transmission electron microscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy and integrated energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy system (TEM, HRTEM and EDX, FEI TECNAI G2, USA), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/max-2400, Rigaku), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS and UPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (DRS, Carry 5000, Agilent, USA), steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (PL, FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK).

The three-electrode system electrochemical workstation (CS350H, Wuhan) was used to measure the photoelectrochemical properties of the catalyst, with Pt wire as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and FTO glass plate loaded with the catalyst as the working electrode. Light source, 50 W LED lamp, was 5 cm away from the working electrode. The electrolyte was 0.5 mol L−1 Na2SO4 aqueous solution. When measuring the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the 10 mV AC voltage was applied, and the frequency range was 100 kHz–0.01 Hz. The Mott-Schottky curve was obtained at the fixed frequency of 1000 Hz, and the scanning rate was 10 mV s−1. The photocurrent was measured at a fixed bias voltage (1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl), and the transient photocurrent (TPC) spectrum was performed. The working electrode was prepared by scraping the corresponding powder samples on FTO via doctor blade technique (S1) (Farooq et al., 2020; Le et al., 2021).

The OFL solution with the concentration of 10 mg/L was selected as the target pollutant system, and the photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples was investigated. Firstly, the 30 mg samples were dispersed in the 50 mL OFL solution. Then the mixed solution was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to meet the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, the 50 W energy-saving LED lamp with the main wavelength of 475 nm was used as the visible light source to irradiate the mixed solution. At regular intervals, the 2 mL solution was taken from the reaction system, filtered through 0.22 μm nylon membrane and detected. The concentration of the organic pollutant in the solution was examined as a function of the irradiation time (Nawaz et al., 2021). The above experiments were repeated to obtain the standard deviation of the kinetic data. The pH values of suspensions were adjusted to 3.00 ± 0.10, 5.00 ± 0.10, 7.00 ± 0.10, 9.00 ± 0.10 and 11.00 ± 0.10, respectively, using diluted HCl or NaOH (0.1 M).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive oxygen species determination and capture experiment
ce:section.ce:para: In the OFL solution with initial pH 3.0, 7.0 and 9.0, respectively, the reactive species trapping experiments were conducted to explore the contribution of various reactive oxygen species (ROS) to OFL degradation in photocatalytic system. The scavengers of photogenerated electron (e−), photogenerated hole (h+), superoxide anion radical (·O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) were potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), furfuryl alcohol (FFA) and isopropanol (IPA), respectively. EPR experiments were conducted on a Bruker A300–9.5/12 (Germany) spectrometer using 5.5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidone (TEMP) as the spin-trapping agent. Specifically, 10 μL DMPO was added into 1 mL suspension contained methanol/ultrapure water and BCF-10. Similarly, 10 μL TEMP was added into 1 mL suspension contained ultrapure water and BCF-10. Subsequently, the DMPO-adducts and TEMP-adducts were respectively detected in dark and after 10 min of visible LED light irradiation.

The residual concentration of OFL was determined by UHPLC (Ultimate 3000, Dionex, USA) with a RS diode array detector and Accucore XL C18 column. The mobile phase was acetonitrile and 1‰ trifluoroacetic acid (20: 80/v: v), and the flow rate was 0.8 mL/min. The detection wavelength was 285 nm and the injection volume was 10 μL.
The changes of total organic carbon (TOC) were recorded by a total organic carbon analyzer (VarioTOC, Elementary Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany).

To investigate the microscopic morphology of the photocatalysts, SEM images of BiVO4, BiVO4/CQDs, β-FeOOH and BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH were obtained, as described in Fig. S1. BiVO4 was stacked in a plate-like structure with a particle diameter of 0.2–0.5 μm (Fig. S1a). However, no obviously diverse of CQDs were detected for CQDs/BiVO4 (Fig. S1b), possibly resulting from the ultrafine size of decorated CQDs (Zhu et al., 2017). Fig. S1c showed that the prepared β-FeOOH powder had a rod-like structure and the length of the aggregates was about 0.7 μm. Coupled with the above analysis, the combination of rod and plate in the BCF-10 samples (Fig. S1d) indicated that there was the electron affinity between the two materials.

TEM and HRTEM images further confirmed the microstructure and crystalline structure of BCF-10 (Fig. 1 ). From Fig. 1a–c, it was observed that the lattice spacing of 0.473 nm matched well with the (011) planes of monoclinic BiVO4 (Gao et al., 2014). The lattice spacing about 0.320 nm was indexed to the CQDs (002) plane (Nan et al., 2015), evidently suggesting the successful introduction of CQDs. Moreover, it could be measured that the lattice spacing of 0.161 nm was the characteristic lattice of β-FeOOH (521) plane (Tang et al., 2013). It was worth mentioning that the lattice edge of BCF-10 changed into blurred and disordered, which was on account of the destruction of the BiVO4 surface structure by the surface oxygen vacancy existence (Hu et al., 2020). Besides, the EDX-mapping of BCF-10 was carried out (Fig. 1d–k). The results further confirmed the existence of Bi, V, Fe, O and C elements.

The chemical composition and crystal structure of photocatalysts were analyzed by XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a). It could be seen that the obtained BiVO4 was perfect crystalline and all the identifiable diffraction peaks could be indexed to monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No.14-0688) (Chen et al., 2019). The characteristic peak of BiVO4 after compounding with CQDs did not change significantly compared with those of pristine BiVO4 possibly because of the small amounts of decorated CQDs. In addition, due to the uniform dispersion and relatively low diffraction intensity of CQDs in BiVO4/CQDs, there was no other diffraction peak (Qian et al., 2016). After the β-FeOOH deposition, the peaks at 11.8°, 26.7° and 55.8° in the XRD patterns of the BCF-10 samples were assigned to the (110), (310) and (521) planes of β-FeOOH (JCPDS NO. 34–1266) (Feng et al., 2016), which indicated that the high crystallinity of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH in BCF-10 provided a structural foundation for its excellent photocatalytic performance.

The elements construction of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH was investigated by XPS. As expected, the XPS scan survey spectra showed the existence of Bi, V, O, Fe and C elements in the sample (Fig. S2). The two peaks of high-resolution Bi 4f region at 164.33 eV (Bi 4f5/2) and 159.0 eV (Bi 4f7/2), together with the two peaks of high-resolution V 2p spectrum at 524.0 eV (V 2p1/2) and 516.6 eV (V 2p3/2) were verified as typical characteristic peaks of BiVO4 (Fig. 2b and c). The C 1s spectra (Fig. 2d) was assigned to three characteristic peaks, corresponding to the non-oxidized cyclic C species (284.8 eV, including C–C, CC and C–H), the C–O or C–OH group in C–O–C (286.1 eV), and the carbonyl C species (288.1 eV) in CO. Considering the hydrothermal reduction process, the peak intensity of oxygen-containing groups (e.g. C–OH, C–O–C and CO) should be weaker than that of non-oxidized C species (e.g. C–C, CC and C–H) (Wang et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2015). The above results confirmed that the CQDs were loaded on the BiVO4. Subsequently, the High-resolution O 1s spectrum would be discussed in detail (Fig. 2e). The O 1s spectra fitted into several O species with different binding energy at 529.3 eV, 530.2 eV and 531.7 eV, which were attributed to the O2− species in the lattice (OL), the hydroxyl group on the metal cation in the oxygen vacancy (OV) and the oxygen in the chemisorbed or dissociated water molecule (OC), respectively (Wang et al., 2018; Jin et al., 2021). The existence of OV was beneficial to reducing the adsorption energy of dissolved oxygen and H2O molecules on the material surface, accordingly promoting the transfer of the electrons to the surface adsorption species. This phenomenon substantially impacted upon photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants (Ge et al., 2019a). Fig. 2f demonstrated that the Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks could be deconvoluted into two peaks, of which 710.8 eV and 724.2 eV for Fe2+, 712.8 eV and 728.4 eV for Fe3+, respectively, which indicated that the Fe element chiefly existed as β-FeOOH in BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH (Tang et al., 2013; Xia et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs and β-FeOOH content on BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To get the optimal content of CQDs or β-FeOOH on BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH, the effect of different CQDs and β-FeOOH content on photocatalytic activity were compared in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, respectively. To start with, three kinds of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH photocatalysts with different CQDs content were prepared. Referring to the synthesis method in section 2.2.2, the CQDs aqueous solutions of 1 mL, 3 mL and 5 mL were added in the process of material synthesis. For comparison purposes, they were named 1-BCF-10, BCF-10 and 5-BCF-10 respectively. Comparing the three curves (Fig. 3a), it could be found that the removal efficiencies of the three materials to OFL were all more than 98% in 20 min, but the degradation rates were dissimilar. Therefore, through fitting the degradation kinetic curve of OFL by first-order kinetic model, the photocatalytic activity of above materials could be distinguished with the rate constant.

As shown in Fig. 3d, the rate constant of photocatalytic degradation of BCF-10 could reach 0.25 min−1, illustrated its optimal photocatalytic activity. Some recent studies indicated that conjugate system existed in CQDs, which enabled CQDs to act as an electron acceptor, thus hindering the recombination of e−/h+ pairs (Asor et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2013). The presence of a handful of CQDs allowed efficient transport of photoelectrons and effective separation of e−/h+ pairs. While excessive CQDs content limited the photocatalytic activity which might root in that dispersed CQDs on the catalyst surface prevented BiVO4 from absorbing visible light (Di et al., 2016). Furthermore, BCF-10 with 0.1 g β-FeOOH exhibited superior photocatalytic performance, suggesting that an appropriate β-FeOOH content promoted the separation and restrained the recombination of photoexcited e−/h+ pairs (Fig. 3b, e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of OFL by different photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the superiority and the pollutant remediation potential of the novel photocatalyst, a controlled experiment for OFL removal with different photocatalysts was carried out. Fig. 3c displayed the photodegradation curve of β-FeOOH, BiVO4, BiVO4/CQDs and BCF-10 to OFL respectively in accordance with the first-order kinetic model. Pristine β-FeOOH had almost no photocatalytic activity for OFL. In order to compare the photodegradation efficiencies of BiVO4, BiVO4/CQDs, BiVO4/β-FeOOH and BCF-10, the rate constants were calculated as 0.13 min−1, 0.18 min−1, 0.20 min−1 and 0.25 min−1, respectively. The results showed that while building a heterojunction between β-FeOOH and BiVO4, the photodegradation rate of OFL could be deeply accelerated by the introduction of CQDs as an electron transfer channel. After irradiating 15 min, the removal efficiency of OFL by BCF-10 could reach 99.21%. Combined with the results of TOC removal efficiency (Fig. S3), the TOC removal efficiency at 20, 40 and 60 min was 15.41%, 37.63% and 68.40%, respectively. Markedly, it could be inferred that the mineralization degree increased with the extension of time. For contrast, there was almost no OFL removal under LED visible light in the absence of photocatalyst (Fig. S4). In addition, in order to eliminate the effect of adsorption, the dark adsorption-desorption equilibrium experiment in dark was investigated. Fig. S4 showed that the adsorption-desorption of OFL by BCF-10 reached equilibrium after 20 min with an adsorption efficiency of about 5%. The photocatalytic degradation constant of this ternary composite was higher than that of other previous literature on OFL degradation (Table S4). Also, the usage of visible light benefited the energy saving. In short, BCF-10 composite represented superior performance and great potential for the OFL photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of BCF-10 dosage and solution pH on photodegradation of OFL
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of BCF-10 dosage on OFL photocatalytic reaction was investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 a–c. The results exhibited that 0.6 g/L photocatalyst was the optimum dosage with the removal efficiency of 99.21% after illumination for 15 min. For one thing, a decrease in the amount of photocatalyst was accompanied by a decrease of available active centers, resulting in a decline in degradation performance. And for another thing, on account of the effects of light scattering and shielding, excessive photocatalysts restricted the utilization of light source (Li et al., 2019).

The solution pH was essential to the photodegradation process on OFL. Generally, the surface charge characteristics of catalysts, pollutants properties and the species of ROS extremely affected the photocatalytic performance under different pH conditions (Jiang et al., 2019). Fig. 4d–f showed the effect of solution pH from 3.0 to 11.0 on OFL degradation. Fig. 4f presented that the rate contents were 0.0414 min−1 and 0.01336 min−1 at pH 3.0 and 11.0. The terribly poor photocatalytic activity of BCF-10 in strong acid and alkali environments was ascribed to the reduction of OFL adsorption owing to the strong electrostatic repulsion. The point of zero charge of BCF-10 sample was measured by pH drift method (S2). As shown in Fig. S5, the unique isoelectric point of BCF-10 was about 5.7. Meanwhile, the pKa of OFL were 6.05 and 8.11, respectively (Kaur et al., 2018). Therefore, at pH 3.0, OFL existed in the form of OFL+, while positive charges gathered on the BCF-10 surface, leading to strong repulsion between the material and the pollutant. In the same way, the phenomenon at pH 11.0 was explained. At pH 5.0, the photodegradation efficiency was improved slightly, which could be illuminated by the fact that when the pH value was close to the IEP of BiVO4, the electrostatic repulsion between the catalyst and OFL seriously weaken caused by less negative charge that BCF-10 carried. When the value of pH was further increased to 7.0, OFL was in existence in amphoteric ions form. At this point, the positively charged hydrogen on piperazine group and the negatively charged catalyst surface generated electrostatic attraction, in order that the photocatalytic performance ameliorated significantly (Wammer et al., 2013). Accordingly, BCF-10 exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance at pH 7.0. However, the photocatalytic performance still displayed a slightly strengthened trend at pH 9.0. Combined with the results of trapping experiments and EPR experiments (Section 3.7), it was found that the main reactive species of OFL degradation changed from 1O2 to ·O2 − when the solution changed from acidic to alkaline. Since the generally applicable performance of ·O2 − was superior than that of 1O2, ·O2 − exhibited powerful activity in the degradation of OFL (Ekande et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020). Therefore, this phenomenon attributable to the increase of activity of reactive species exceeded the inhibition caused by electrostatic repulsion. Furthermore, at pH 11.0, the combination of h+ with excessive OH− (Eqs. (1)–(3)) was also a substantial reason for the strong inhibition of OFL removal (Wang et al., 2019). The terrific removal efficiency of OFL by BCF-10 at pH 5.0, 7.0 and 9.0 demonstrated that BCF-10 had an admirable practical application prospect, irrespective of weak alkaline and acid environments.
(1) h+ + OH− →·OH
(2) ·OH + OH− → H2O + O·
(3) 4h+ + 4OH− → O2 + 2H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BCF-10 in actual water
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the Yellow River and Lanzhou tap water were studied as typical samples of surface water and water supply system respectively. The application feasibility of BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH was evaluated by two kinds of actual water samples. Table S2 and Table S3 listed the physicochemical properties of these two water samples and the background concentrations of OFL, respectively. In the experiments, the initial concentration of OFL was 10 mg/L, and the background concentration of OFL in the Yellow River and tap water could be ignored. All the collected water samples must be filtered and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

As shown in Fig. S6, when the irradiation time was extended to 100 min, the removal effectives of OFL in the Yellow River and tap water reached 94.71% and 97.61% respectively, indicating that BCF-10 retained a satisfied prospect in practical application. By comparison with ultrapure water, the degradation rates of OFL in actual water was slightly limited possibly resulting from the combined action of coexisting inorganic ions and organic compounds (Wang et al., 2019). From Fig. S7, it could be seen that the common anions in actual water including Cl−, SO4 2− and NO3 − had no palpable influence on the OFL removal. Fig. S8 revealed that HA, as a reference for NOM, also had no significant effect on the photocatalytic degradation of OFL. Generally, NOM had dual effects on the removal of pollutants in water: i) the inhibitory effect was due to the shading effect of NOM and the quenching of free radicals (Maoz and Chefetz, 2010), ii) the promoting effect caused by excited NOM or reactive oxygen species produced by photoexcitation (Zepp and Schlotzhauer, 1981). Therefore, the inconspicuous effect of NOM on the degradation of OFL might be attributed to the mutual cancellation of the above two factors.

To evaluate the prospect for the practical application, the stability and reusability of the photocatalyst were realized by cycling experiments. Five cycling experiments were carried out under the identical experimental conditions as the OFL degradation by BCF-10. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was centrifuged, rinsed with ultrapure water for 3 times and dried for recovery. As shown in Fig. S9a, the prepared BCF-10 had satisfactory stability and performance, and the removal efficiency of OFL was always above 99%. Surprisingly, an increase in the rate constant with increasing number of cycles was observed (Fig. S10). This exciting phenomenon that will be elucidated later. The microstructural stability of BCF-10 was further investigated by XRD in Fig. S9b. The results illustrated that there was no obvious change in the XRD peaks of the reused BCF-10, which verified the wonderful stability of the prepared photocatalyst. Overall, BCF-10 could retain amended photocatalytic activity and strong material stability, and had the potential of recovery and reuse.

In order to further explore the factor for the wonderful stability of BCF-10 and the photodegradation mechanism of OFL, the surface chemical state of BCF-10 before and after reaction were compared through XPS analysis. From Fig. S9c, it was clearly seen that the relative intensity of OV peak of BCF-10 after photodegrading (BCF-10-used) was significantly higher than that of fresh BCF-10, suggesting that there was a diffusion of surface oxygen vacancies. Meanwhile, the alteration of the four deconvolution peaks in Fe 2p was observed, which was attributed to the mutual conversion between Fe2+ and Fe3+ in the photocatalysis process. Coupled with the obvious decrease in the relative intensity of OL peak, it could conclude that electron transfer occurred between Fe2+/Fe3+ and lattice oxygen, leading to a gradual augment of oxygen vacancies on the photocatalysis surface. Interestingly, Raman spectra of BCF-10 (used at pH 3.0, 7.0 and 9.0) exhibited typical vibrational bands of fresh BCF-10 specimens (Fig. S11) with slight peak shift and intensity attenuation after photocatalytic reaction, which was associated with the crystalline degradation caused by the formation of OV (Yao et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2021). This was also a microcosmic explanation of outstanding reusability of BCF-10. It was understandable that the low-coordination metal centers could enter the surface oxygen vacancy, which was profitable to the chemisorption and activation of O2 and H2O molecules (Liu et al., 2017). This also corresponded to the enhancement of the OC peak of BCF-10-used in Fig. S9c. Therefore, the OV in β-FeOOH could not only multiply the active sites, but optimize the adsorption and activation behaviors, which improved the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst effectively. Notably, compared with the fresh BCF-10, the Bi 4f and Fe 2p peaks of BCF-10-used slightly shifted 0.2 eV and 0.3 eV, respectively (Fig. S9d, f), which was due to the change in chemical coordination of metal cations unbound by surface oxygen vacancies (Liu et al., 2019a,b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical and photoelectric properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–vis DRS) and photoluminescence spectrum (PL) could characterize the charge separation ability of the photocatalyst, which was closely related to the photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. S12a, the absorption edge of BiVO4/CQDs was 531 nm, which had no obvious red-shifted compared to pristine BiVO4 (528 nm). The results presented that the light absorption range of BiVO4 would not be expanded by the single load of CQDs. The response spectrum of BCF-10 extended from 531 nm to 562 nm. Furthermore, the brilliant visible-light absorption of β-FeOOH resulted in a stronger light absorption intensity of BCF-10 primarily in visible region than that of BiVO4, suggesting that the introduction of β-FeOOH improved the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4/CQDs. The optical band gap of semiconductors could be estimated by the following Eq. (4) (Liu et al., 2015):
(4) (αhν) = A (hν - Eg) n/2
where α, h, v, A and Eg are expressed as absorption coefficient, Planck constant, optical frequency, constant and band gap energy, respectively. The n value is determined by the type of optical transition of semiconductors (n = 1, direct transition; n = 4, indirect transition) (Huang et al., 2013). When using n = 1, an excellent linear fitting was shown in Fig. S12b, which was consistent with previous studies which claimed that BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were direct transition semiconductors (Lima et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2015). Consequently, from the Tauc plot curve, it could be calculated that the direct band gap of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were 2.42 eV and 2.18 eV respectively.

Fig. 5 a showed the PL spectra of obtained the samples. The weaker fluorescence emission intensity meant the higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency (Wei et al., 2018). Hence the emission peak of BiVO4/CQDs was obviously lower than that of BiVO4 which manifested that the effect of CQDs as an electron transfer channel to enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers was validated preliminarily. When β-FeOOH was loaded, the fluorescence intensity of the photocatalyst was the weakest, which might be related to the formation of heterojunction between β-FeOOH and BiVO4/CQDs (He et al., 2015).

The charge separation and electron transfer kinetics of the prepared samples were ulteriorly researched via transient photocurrent (TPC) spectra, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). In the TPC spectrum shown in Fig. 5b, the entire prepared photoanodes showed the highest photocurrent density of BCF-10 at six switching cycles. The appearance of the photocurrent transient spikes might be caused by severe recombination of electrons and holes accumulated at the moment of turning on the light (Ge et al., 2019b). Concurrently, compared with the photocurrent of BiVO4/β-FeOOH and BCF-10, it could be demonstrated that the boost of photocurrent of BCF-10 was not just depended on the heterojunction construction, and at the meantime, the load of CQDs played a significant role similarly. Moreover, comparing the Nyquist curves of several photoanodes under light and dark conditions (Fig. 5c and Fig. S13), we observed that the arc radius of BCF-10 under light was far smaller than that of other samples and much smaller than its own arc radius under dark conditions. This accounted for the fact that illuminations activated the electron transfer behavior in heterojunctions, thus descending the internal resistance of photocatalyst (Chen et al., 2016). The arc radius of BiVO4/β-FeOOH was larger than that of BCF-10, which indicated that CQDs might be used as an electron transfer channel to decrease the resistance between BiVO4 and β-FeOOH. As shown in Fig. 5d, the photocurrent-potential curve clearly exhibited the current density of the samples from 0.016 mA cm2 (at 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl) of BiVO4 to 0.065 mA cm2 (at 1.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl) of BCF-10. When the photocurrent curve raised steeply at ∼0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), the existence of the inflection points was on account of the acceleration of carrier separation efficiency. Compared with BiVO4/CQDs and BiVO4/β-FeOOH, the inflection point of BCF-10 had a noticeably cathode shifts (200–300 mV), and the photocurrent curve was steeper (Liu et al., 2019a,b). The above experimental results of photochemistry evidenced that the joint construction between electron transfer channel and heterojunction successfully ameliorated the charge transfer kinetics and the separation efficiency of e−/h+ pairs (Mao et al., 2019; Tang et al., 2013; Xie et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2020).

The photocatalytic effect was realized by the redox reaction caused by photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) on the heterogeneous solid surface of the photocatalyst (Nosaka and Nosaka, 2017). Previously, studies on the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of organic pollutants primarily focused on the band structure of photocatalyst and the degradation intermediates of pollutants (Albini and Monti, 2003; Cheng et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2017b). As reactive species were the foremost intermediates of photocatalytic reaction, the identification and evaluation of reactive species were of great significance for understanding photodegradation mechanism, improving photodegradation efficiency and accelerating the practical application of photocatalysts.

To investigate the action mechanism of reactive species in the photocatalysis process, the trapping experiments of several reactive species under acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions (pH = 3.0, 7.0 and 9.0) were carried out with pKa1 and pKa2 of OFL as the dividing point (pKa1 = 6.05, pKa2 = 8.11). The formation path of reactive species was always based on the results of trapping experiments.

At pH 7.0, EDTA-2Na almost completely inhibited the photocatalytic degradation of OFL, while in the presence of BQ and K2Cr2O7, the rate constants decreased by 63.36% and 58.72% (Fig. 6 d–f). Thereafter, the dissolved oxygen in the solution was removed by nitrogen with 350 mL/min for 30 min. The decrease in dissolved oxygen, a precursor of ·O2 −, significantly inhibited the degradation of OFL (Fig. 6). The results depicted that h+, e− and ·O2 − were involved in the degradation of OFL at pH 7.0, and e− induced the production of ·O2 − according to the reaction Eq. (5).
(5) O2 + e− = ·O2 −

In the solution of pH 9.0, there was virtually no change in the OFL degradation with IPA and FFA. On the contrary, the existence of EDTA-2Na, K2Cr2O7 and BQ restrained the degradation rate dramatically (Fig. 6g–i), indicating that h+, e− and ·O2 − still played major roles. In addition, under alkaline condition, the contribution proportion of h+ to photocatalysis caused by OH− and h+ reaction was weaker than that under neutral condition, which was consistent with the conclusion in section 3.3.3.

When pH was 3.0, h+ still was the dominant reactive species to OFL degradation. However, we noticed an attractive phenomenon that the rate constant was decreased when the addition of FFA under acidic conditions, while the presence of BQ had no noticeably influence (Fig. 6a–c). The above revealed that the leading reactive species had changed from ·O2 − to 1O2. Through consulting the literature, it was discovered that only a few studies claimed that the production of 1O2 would increase when the external environment changes from neutral to acidic, but no explanation had been given (Ding et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2020). Simultaneously, there was no report that the species of ROS formed in the photodegradation of pollutants would alter with the change of pH in the reaction system.

According to related researches, the formation mechanism of 1O2 could be divided into three categories: (I) the formation of 1O2 via stimulating the energy transfer of organic molecules to the triple ground state of O2 (Nosaka and Nosaka, 2017); (II) metal oxides release metal ions to form 1O2 under acidic conditions (Lu et al., 2020); (III)·O2 − as a precursor to form 1O2 (Rajh et al., 2014b). As shown in Fig. S4, it could be seen that there was no photosensitive self-degradation of OFL in 20 min, which was due to the fact that the light emitted by LED light source was not enough to excite OFL molecules to form 3OFL∗. Meantime, the adsorption-desorption curve of BCF-10 to OFL illustrated that the OFL removal using BCF-10 in the dark was a typical adsorption process, and the adsorption rate was only 5%. Hence mechanism I and II were excluded. The possibility of producing 1O2 via ·O2 − was the utmost (Eq. (6)).
(6) ·O2 − + ·O2H + H+ → 1O2 +H2O2

However, the results of trapping experiments at pH 3.0 were unexpected. As a scavenger of ·O2 −, the addition of BQ did not significantly vary the degradation rate constant. Namely, the trapping of ·O2 −, the precursor of 1O2, was unacted on formation of 1O2. This bizarre phenomenon was due to an interesting mechanism. The reaction rate constant of BQ with ·O2 − was k· O2 − /BQ = 0.9 × 109 M−1s−1 as trapping agent (Yang et al., 2021), and the rate constant of Eq. (6) was k (6) = 2.4 × 1012−pH M−1s−1 (Nosaka et al., 2017). When pH = 3, the result of k(6) > k· O2 − /BQ clearly suggested that ·O2 − gave priority to the formation of 1O2 under acidic conditions, which explained the above experimental phenomena. The conclusion that the reactive species would have directional transformation under acidic conditions not simply provided a unique perspective for exploring the impact of solution pH on the degradation process, but offered a novel idea for the selective utilization of reactive species. Considering the oxidizability of K2Cr2O7 under acidic conditions, the experiment with AgNO3 as an electron quenchant was supplemented (Zhao et al., 2021). Fig. 6 showed that the degradation of OFL was slightly inhibited after the electron was quenched. This further evidenced that quenching electrons, as the precursor of ·O2 −, could indirectly affect the formation of 1O2.

EPR experiments were employed to evaluate and confirm the existence and transformation of three ROSs (Fig. S14). Firstly, we noticed that the signal of DMPO-·OH could not be measured at pH 3.0 and 7.0, which testified the results of the trapping experiments, and indicated that no ·OH was generated in the photocatalysis process in the meantime (Fig. S14a, d). Then, in comparison with the background noise at pH 7.0, the ESR spectrum with characteristic signal of 1:1:1 displayed a strong TEMP-1O2 signal at pH 3.0 under visible light (Fig. S14b, e). Lastly, we observed the quadruple characteristic peak of ·O2 − complexed with DMPO at pH 7.0, but this peak disappeared at pH 3.0, and only appeared three miscellaneous peaks (Fig. S14c, f). The above results illustrated that there was a directional transformation of ·O2 − to 1O2 during the transition from neutral to acidic in the photocatalytic system.

According to the UV–Vis DRS spectra of the prepared samples, the band gap of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH had been calculated to be 2.42 eV and 2.18 eV in section 3.6 (Fig. S12b), respectively. Consequently, in order to further identify the band structure of the photocatalyst, the Mott-Schottky tests were measured (Fig. 7 a). To start with, since the carrier density was inversely proportional to the slope of the C−2-E curve, the slope of the BCF-10 curve was higher than that of other samples, which meant that the carrier density of BCF-10 was considerably higher compared to BiVO4/CQDs and β-FeOOH (Zhou et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the slope comparison between BiVO4/CQDs and pure BiVO4 showed that the introduction of CQDs boosted the carrier density obviously. Meanwhile, the positive slope of the curve in Fig. 7a evidenced that BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were typical n-type semiconductors. In addition, the flat-band potentials (EFB) of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were obtained according to the tangent relations of Mott-Schottky curves. The abscissa of the intersection between the tangent and the X axis determined that the EFB of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were −0.12 V and −0.49 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively, and the EFB of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were calculated to be 0.08 V and −0.30 V (vs. NHE) via Eq. (7) (Tang et al., 2018), respectively. Fig. S15 exhibited the UPS spectra of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH. By identifying the difference between the excitation energy (21.22 eV) and the peak width, the VB energy of BiVO4 was determined to be 6.78 eV. Since the VB energy calculated by UPS spectrum was 4.44 V higher than that of ordinary hydrogen electrode (NHE), the VB of BiVO4 corresponds to 2.34 V (vs. NHE) (Chen et al., 2020). Accordingly, its CB was located at −0.08 V (vs. NHE) on account of its band gap of 2.42 eV. For the β-FeOOH sample, CB and VB were located at −0.4 V and 1.78 V (vs. NHE), respectively.
(7) E(NHE) = E(Ag / AgCl) + 0.197 eV

Derived from the results of Mott-Schottky, UV–Vis DRS and trapping experiments, a reasonable S-scheme reaction mechanism was proposed (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. S12b, the introduction of CQDs could not regulate the band gap of BiVO4, which indicated that CQDs perhaps did not construct the heterojunction with BiVO4. In comparison to BiVO4, the EFB of BiVO4/CQDs had a negative shift of about 0.06 eV, which was owing to that the electron storage and electron-rich characteristics of CQDs that cause the migration of electrons from BiVO4 to CQDs to form electron aggregation, thus making the flat-band potential more negative (Zhang et al., 2018). However, the ECB of the samples would not be changed. In addition, the electrochemical measurement determined that the introduction of CQDs reduced the electron transfer resistance between BiVO4 and β-FeOOH and significantly improved the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges. CQDs could not affect the band structure while greatly could promote the photocatalytic activity of BCF-10. Therefore, it could be verified that CQDs were not involved in the construction of heterojunctions, and its main function was to improve the separation efficiency of e−/h+ as an electronic channel.

The work functions of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH could be verified by calculating the difference between the cut-off energy and the excitation energy in the UPS spectrum (Baddorf et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S15, the corresponding cut-off energies were 16.82 eV and 16.94 eV, thus the work functions of BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were −0.04 eV and −0.16 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Furthermore, the flat-band potential of BiVO4 was more positive than that of β-FeOOH (Fig. 7a), which further proved that the latter had a higher Fermi level. When BiVO4 closely contacted with β-FeOOH, the free electrons in β-FeOOH would spontaneously transfer to BiVO4 through the interface until their Fermi levels were aligned due to the lower EFB and Fermi level of BiVO4 than β-FeOOH (Fig. 7a) (Nosaka et al., 2017). In the equilibrium, the electron density on the β-FeOOH interface was declined and the electrons were amassed at the BiVO4 interface, resulting in the upward bending band on β-FeOOH and the downward bending band on BiVO4, thus a built-in electric field pointing from β-FeOOH to BiVO4 was established on the interface (Fig. 7b). Under irradiation, the electrons in BiVO4 and β-FeOOH were excited from VB to CB, respectively. The electric field and curved band structure formed at the interface could drive the photogenerated holes of β-FeOOH to complex with photogenerated electrons of BiVO4, leaving photogenerated electrons and holes in the CB of β-FeOOH and the VB of BiVO4, respectively. Obviously, such a charge transfer mode was described to a step scheme (S-scheme) appropriately (Cheng et al., 2021). Based on the above explanation, it could be demonstrated that the main contribution of CQDs was to transfer photogenerated electrons in semiconductor heterojunction system. In conclusion, the combined action of CQDs, internal electric field and band bending was the driving force for the recombination of CB electrons of BiVO4 and VB holes of β-FeOOH. The strong oxidizability of VB holes in BiVO4 and the strong reducibility of CB electrons in β-FeOOH were simultaneously preserved via S-scheme heterojunctions. Hence BCF-10 provided sufficient electronic reducibility to satisfy the production of ·O2 − (O2/·O2 − = −0.33 V vs. NHE) while oxidizing pollutants directly via h+.

In this work, we embedded CQDs on the surface of monoclinic scheelite phase BiVO4, and successfully prepared BiVO4/CQDs/β-FeOOH S-scheme heterojunction with β-FeOOH by electrostatic self-assembly. SEM, HRTEM, EDX-mapping and XRD analysis demonstrated that the lamellar BiVO4 and rod β-FeOOH in the ternary composite photocatalyst BCF-10 combined closely by electrostatic attraction and crystallized completely. XPS analysis showed that plenty of oxygen vacancies existed on the surface of BCF-10, and Fe elements coexisted in the form of Fe (II) and Fe (III). Compared with BiVO4 and BiVO4/CQDs, BCF-10 exhibited improved photocatalytic performance. The degradation efficiency of OFL in 15 min was 99.21% and the mineralization efficiency was 68.40% within 60 min. Photoelectrochemical analysis illustrated that BCF-10 had superior photocurrent response and diminutive electrochemical impedance, indicating that the photogenerated carriers in BCF-10 had been effectively separated and transferred. The eminent photocatalytic activity and charge separation efficiency of the BCF-10 were attributed to the synergistic effect of S-scheme heterojunction, CQDs as well as surface oxygen vacancy. After five photodegradation cycles, the gradual increased rate constant in photocatalytic degradation was perceived, which was resulting from the regeneration behaviors of surface oxygen vacancies. And this showed that BCF-10 had perfect reusability. In particular, BCF-10 still had outstanding degradation performance in actual water photocatalytic systems including Yellow River water and tap water. Combined with the results of cycling experiments, BCF-10 had a certain practical application prospect. Based on the comprehensive analysis of the results of solution pH effect experiments, trapping experiments and EPR experiments, a possible pathway of reactive species transformation was proposed, that was, ·O2 − was inclined to produce 1O2 with low oxidation activity under acidic condition, rather than directly oxidize pollutants. This work inspires the further construction of high-performance photocatalysts with unique S-scheme heterostructures and provides a novel perspective for exploring the role of reactive groups in the photocatalysis process. Inspiration for theoretical studying the effective removal of antibiotics in the water environment will be obtained.

Yuan Wen: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Validation. Zhaowei Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yonghui Cai: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Mengxi Song: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Kemin Qi: Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiaoyun Xie: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
